input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 serial robot with six axes, a manufacturing robotic system where each axis is actuated by specific motors. The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor, and the system operates in industrial robotics with high precision requirements, multi-axis coordination, and real-time control demands for motion accuracy and friction reduction.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT manage system complexity. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is supported via MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed implementation and stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization is maintained through continuous live-data linking between physical and digital components, with live measurements streamed one-way from the physical system to the digital model, ensuring data-driven representations without bidirectional communication as per the digital shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is orchestrated via a Digital Shadow Reference Model implemented as a UML class diagram, comprising components such as Asset (physical entity), DataTrace (data points), Processing (computational logic), and Model (engineering/simulation). The Processing component consumes input data traces using simulation or engineering models to produce output data, with traceability maintained through 'originates from' associations for transitive lineage of data transformations.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Data is streamed via MQTT protocol, utilizing Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for interoperability and integrating with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for real-time control and data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for modeling functional and logical architectures, as well as the product/technical layer. Behavioral aspects are captured using state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, while analytical models utilize MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java. Data originates from physical objects through automated one-way data flows, managed by integrating heterogeneous modeling notations and addressing stakeholder perspectives across multiple abstraction levels.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series data from physical sensors are streamed via MQTT protocol, including target and actual position values, their derivatives, and motor current measurements, as part of a one-way data flow characteristic of a digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor time series data; network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a four-step methodology: Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification. This methodology is applied in Section IV.A to analyze robot motion accuracy by processing sensor data to detect friction loss and generate insights for decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides insights on joint friction through dashboard visualization and traceability of relationships between model elements and physical measurements to support decision-making processes.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system utilizes industry standards such as SysML for systems engineering modeling, AADL for architecture description, ACME as a framework for architecture description languages, MontiArc for component-based modeling, and Simulink for simulation. Methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied to manage complexity across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) during system development.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

While the system is used for understanding friction and improving accuracy, the concept of 'dt_services' as a specific characteristic is not detailed in the provided text.

The description is highly accurate and detailed, directly supported by Section II.A ('Modeling Software and Systems'). It correctly lists MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), and behavior modeling tools (MontiArc Automaton, Simulink, C++, Java). Quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

The description provides a comprehensive and detailed summary of the Digital Shadow Reference Model described in the text. It correctly identifies the UML class diagram implementation, lists the key components (Asset, DataTrace, Processing, Model), and accurately describes the 'originates from' association for traceability.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific external systems, standards, and protocols used for integration, including the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, the MQTT protocol for streaming, and the use of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for interoperability.

The extraction correctly identifies MQTT as the communication protocol used for sensor data. It accurately states that other technical details like network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the provided text. This is supported by the quote: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' The document does not contain further network infrastructure details.

The extraction correctly identifies that this characteristic is not in the document, as the text does not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational requirements, or scalability mechanisms.

While it mentions 'development phase' in the context of system modeling (SysML) and 'operating time' for the robot, it does not describe the full lifecycle stages of the Digital Twin itself (e.g., design, build, operate, maintain/decommission), nor does it cover transition management or lifecycle governance approaches as defined by the characteristic.
